title: Binary Search
description: Master the universal binary search template, parametric search on answers, and STL bounds to transform brute-force solutions into O(log n) elegance on Codeforces.

sections.content: Binary search is one of the most recognisable patterns in competitive programming — and also one of the most misapplied. Beyond searching in a sorted array, the real power lies in **binary searching on the answer itself**: any time the feasibility of a value is monotonic (once X works, every value > X also works — or vice-versa), you can binary search. Problems rated 1200–1800 on Codeforces routinely hide this pattern behind phrases like *'minimise the maximum'*, *'maximise the minimum'*, or *'can you achieve X in at most K steps?'* Recognising that signal and setting up a correct `check()` function is the entire skill.

Two styles dominate contest code: the **closed-interval** style `[lo, hi]` with `while (lo <= hi)`, and the **half-open** style `[lo, hi)` with `while (lo < hi)`. Both are correct when used consistently. The closed-interval style is slightly easier to reason about for beginners; the half-open style mirrors how `std::lower_bound` works internally. Pick one and commit — mixing them within a single binary search is the root cause of most infinite-loop bugs.

sections.title: Closed-Interval Template — First Index Where Condition Is True

sections.label: The Two Bugs That Kill Binary Search in Contests
sections.content: **Bug 1 — Overflow:** `mid = (lo + hi) / 2` overflows when lo + hi > 2^31 - 1 (common when searching answer ranges up to 10^9 or 10^18). Always write `mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2`. **Bug 2 — Infinite loop:** In a half-open `[lo, hi)` style, writing `hi = mid - 1` instead of `hi = mid` causes an infinite loop when lo and hi differ by 1. Dry-run your template on a 2-element array before every contest.

sections.label: Dry-Run Checklist for Any Binary Search
sections.content: Before submitting, verify your template on three edge cases: (1) the target is the first element, (2) the target is the last element, (3) the target does not exist. These three cases catch 90% of off-by-one bugs without needing to think about invariants abstractly.

The C++ STL provides four binary-search utilities that operate on any **sorted range** in O(log n). They all accept iterators, so they work on `vector`, `array`, and sorted `deque`. For `set` and `map`, use the member `.lower_bound()` method instead — the free-function versions will degrade to O(n) on those containers because they lack random-access iterators.

rows: Insertion point; count of elements < x
End of equal range; count of elements ≤ x
Simple existence check (returns bool, no position)
All occurrences of x at once

sections.title: STL Bounds — Contest-Ready Patterns

sections.content: The most powerful form of binary search in CP does **not** search inside an existing array. Instead, you binary search over the **range of possible answer values**. The template: define `check(mid)` that returns `true` if answer value `mid` is achievable/feasible. If the feasibility function is **monotonic** — once `mid` is feasible, `mid+1` is also feasible (or vice-versa) — binary search finds the boundary in O(log V) calls to `check()`, where V is the answer range. The total complexity is O(check_cost × log V).

sections.title: Parametric Search — Allocate Minimum Pages

sections.label: Four Signals That Scream 'Binary Search on Answer'
sections.content: 1. **Minimise the maximum** value of something across groups/segments. 2. **Maximise the minimum** distance/gap between placed objects. 3. **'Can you do X in at most K moves/operations?'** (check returns bool). 4. The answer is a non-negative integer or real value in a bounded range and the feasibility check is a simple greedy scan. If you see any of these, immediately ask: *Is the check function monotonic?*

Binary search also applies to **continuous domains**. When the answer is a real number, replace the integer loop with a fixed iteration count (100 iterations gives ~30 decimal digits of precision — far more than any judge needs). Do **not** use `while (hi - lo > 1e-9)` as the termination condition; floating-point rounding can cause it to loop forever.

sections.title: Real-Valued Binary Search — Rope Cutting
Finding First and Last Occurrence Without STL

sections.label: Binary Search Requires a Sorted (or Monotonic) Domain
sections.content: Binary search **only works** when the predicate transitions from false to true exactly once across the search space (monotonic). If the answer space has multiple true/false boundaries, binary search will silently return a wrong answer — it won't crash. Always verify monotonicity before applying the pattern.

sections.title: Exercise 1: Tracing lower_bound and upper_bound Arithmetic
sections.explanation:
Vector is {10, 20, 20, 30, 40} at indices 0–4.

**a:** `lower_bound(v, 20)` — first element ≥ 20. That is index 1 (value 20). → **a = 1**.

**b:** `upper_bound(v, 20)` — first element > 20. Index 0 is 10 (≤20), 1 and 2 are 20 (≤20), index 3 is 30 (>20). → **b = 3**.

**c:** `lower_bound(v, 25)` — first element ≥ 25. Index 3 has value 30 ≥ 25. → **c = 3**.

**d:** `lower_bound(v, 50)` — no element ≥ 50 exists; iterator falls off the end = v.begin() + 5. → **d = 5**.

**b - a = 3 - 1 = 2** — this is the count of occurrences of 20 in the vector, a classic trick.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Count Pairs with Sum Strictly Less Than K
sections.approach: Fix i, then count valid j > i: we need arr[j] < k - arr[i]. Since arr is sorted, all valid j's form a contiguous prefix of arr[i+1..n-1]. Use lower_bound to find the first index where arr[j] >= (k - arr[i]) — everything before that index is valid. This is O(n log n) total.
sections.explanation:
For each fixed i, the search range is `arr[i+1 .. n-1]`. We want count of elements strictly less than `limit = k - arr[i]`. `lower_bound` on this subrange returns an iterator to the first element ≥ limit. The number of elements before it is `it - (arr.begin() + i + 1)`.

Correctness: the array is sorted, so all valid j's are a contiguous prefix of the search range — binary search applies cleanly.

**Complexity:** O(n log n) sort + O(n log n) for the loop = **O(n log n)** total. For n = 2×10⁵ this is ~3.4×10⁶ operations — comfortably within a 1-second time limit.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Aggressive Cows (Classic Parametric Search)
sections.approach: Binary search on the answer D (the minimum distance). check(D): greedily place cows — always place the next cow at the earliest stall that is >= D away from the last placed cow. If we can fit all c cows, D is feasible. Feasibility is monotonically decreasing: if D works, D-1 also works. So binary search for the maximum feasible D.
sections.explanation:
**Why greedy check works:** after sorting stalls, to maximise the minimum gap we want to spread cows as far apart as possible. Greedy — place each cow at the earliest available stall respecting the current distance threshold — is provably optimal for this feasibility question.

**Monotonicity:** if distance D is feasible (we can place all c cows), then D-1 is also feasible (relaxing the constraint never reduces feasibility). This guarantees binary search is valid.

**Complexity:** O(n log n) sort + O(log(max_gap)) × O(n) for check = **O(n log V)** where V ≤ 10⁹. For n = 10⁵ this is roughly 5×10⁶ operations.

sections.title: Exercise 4: K-th Smallest in an M×N Multiplication Table
sections.approach: Binary search on the answer value x. For a given x, count how many table entries are <= x: row i contributes min(floor(x/i), N) entries (values i, 2i, ..., up to x capped at Ni). Sum over all rows. Find the smallest x where count(x) >= k.
sections.explanation:
**Key insight:** `countLE(x)` is monotonically non-decreasing in x, so binary search applies. We want the smallest x with countLE(x) >= k.

**Why ans is always in the table:** the binary search lands on the smallest x where the count jumps to >= k. Suppose x is not in the table; then countLE(x) == countLE(x-1) (no new entries introduced at x), so countLE(x-1) >= k too — contradicting x being the minimum. Therefore ans is always an actual table entry.

**countLE derivation:** row i has entries {i, 2i, 3i, ..., Ni}. Entries ≤ x in row i = floor(x/i) but capped at N columns = min(x/i, N).

**Complexity:** O(M × log(M×N)). For M = N = 3×10⁴, log(MN) ≈ 30 → ~9×10⁵ operations.
